does: This paper introduces Human-on-the-Bridge (HOB), a scalable evaluation framework that combines human expertise with automated testing for AI agents operating across multiple turns. HOB places human experts upstream to curate evaluation intelligence—including domain context, adversarial traps, scoring personas, audit rules, and fallback policies—before testing begins. The ProofAgent Harness then executes this curated intelligence repeatedly through multi-turn adversarial interactions, captures detailed execution traces, applies multi-juror scoring, and generates evidence-linked reports. The approach bridges the gap between fragmented evaluation signals: it preserves expert judgment like Human-in-the-Loop review while scaling beyond manual review, structures evaluation more thoroughly than LLM-as-judge methods, and systematizes red teaming and trace auditing into a cohesive pipeline that works across different LLM-based agents.
connects: This work responds to fundamental limitations in evaluating agent systems identified across multiple research threads. Traditional benchmarks measure isolated capabilities but miss agentic behaviors like multi-turn reasoning, tool use, and context preservation. Recent scaling of Human-in-the-Loop evaluation has revealed its bottleneck when applied to complex agent behavior. Parallel work on LLM-as-judge evaluation acknowledged dependence on evaluator design choices. Advances in red teaming and adversarial testing, though valuable, typically remain episodic and disconnected from systematic auditing frameworks. The paper integrates insights from behavioral evaluation, structured adversarial testing, qualitative trace analysis, and multi-annotator agreement paradigms to address how expert knowledge can be captured once, then applied consistently and repeatedly.
enables: HOB enables scalable evaluation of increasingly complex agentic systems by front-loading expert knowledge into reusable evaluation artifacts. This capability supports broader research into agent safety and reliability by reducing the cost of comprehensive evaluation relative to manual review. The approach opens pathways for studying emergent agent behaviors across many evaluation runs while maintaining interpretability through evidence-linked reporting. Future work can extend HOB to multi-agent scenarios, integrate continuous learning from evaluation data, develop domain-specific evaluation libraries, and apply the framework to agents operating in high-stakes domains where transparent, auditable evaluation records are critical. The separation of curation from execution enables rapid iteration on evaluation methodologies and systematic benchmarking of agent improvements over time.
